content:
2 min read
Just now

--

A developer I know sent me his weekend project to look over before he made the repo public. He had built almost the whole thing with Cursor and was proud of how fast it came together. It took me about thirty seconds to find the problem: an OpenAI key in plain text on line three, and ten more secrets scattered across the other files.
He didn’t put them there on purpose. He asked the assistant to add the OpenAI call, and it wired up the client with the key inline. The key was his, pasted in during an earlier prompt, and the model kept reusing it inline every time it touched that file.
The vulnerable code
This is roughly what was in there (CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials):
import openaiopenai.api_key = "sk-proj-9aXfK2mQ8vNpL4rT7wYzB3cD5eF1gH6j"
def summarize(text):
resp = openai.chat.completions.create(
model="gpt-4",
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}]
)
return resp.choices[0].message.content
It runs. Tests pass. It also leaks the moment the repo goes public, and bots scan new GitHub commits for exactly this pattern in seconds.
Why this keeps happening
The model learned from millions of tutorials and StackOverflow answers where the author dropped a literal key inline to keep the example short. That shortcut is everywhere in the training data, so the model treats inline secrets as the normal way to show working code. It has no concept of which strings are sensitive. To the model, an API key is just a string that makes the example complete.
The fix
Pull the secret out of source entirely:
import os
import openaiopenai.api_key = os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"]
Put the real value in a .env file, add .env to .gitignore, and catch mistakes before they ship with a pre-commit hook running gitleaks. Then rotate the key that was already exposed, because once it touches a commit it is burned.
I’ve been running SafeWeave for this. It hooks into Cursor and Claude Code as an MCP server and flags hardcoded secrets before I move on to the next file. That said, even a basic pre-commit hook with gitleaks will catch most of what’s in this post. The important thing is catching it early, whatever tool you use.
